paper_title: Aligning Latent Geometry for Spherical Flow Matching in Image Generation

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Latent flow matching for image generation usually transports Gaussian noise to variational autoencoder latents along linear paths. Both endpoints, however, concentrate in thin spherical shells, and a Euclidean chord leaves those shells even when preprocessing aligns their radii. By decomposing each latent token into radial and angular components, we show through component-swap probes that decoded perceptual and semantic content is carried predominantly by direction, with radius contributing much less. We therefore project data latents onto a fixed token radius, use the radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior, finetune the decoder with the encoder frozen, and replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation. The resulting geodesic paths stay on the sphere at every timestep, and their velocity targets are purely angular by construction. Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers, leaves the diffusion architecture unchanged, and requires no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective.

sections.title:
Aligning Latent Geometry for Spherical Flow
Matching in Image Generation
sections.content: Tuna Han Salih Meral1 Kaan Oktay2 Hidir Yesiltepe1 Adil Kaan Akan2 Pinar Yanardag1 1Virginia Tech 2fal (a) Linear (b) Spherical (Ours) (c) FID, SiT-B/2, FLUX.2 VAE Figure 1: Latent flow matching ignores the geometry of VAE latents. (a) Linear latent flow matching connects Gaussian noise to VAE latent with straight line. Although both endpoints concentrate on thin shells, the line passes through interior radii rarely occupied by either endpoint. (b) We project data latents and sample noise on shared fixed-radius sphere, then train along the spherical arc, or slerp. (c) By aligning the latent geometry to the sphere, our method reaches the vanilla-linear FID= 30 in about 2.2 fewer training steps and continues to improve, without changing the diffusion architecture or adding any auxiliary encoder.

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Latent flow matching for image generation usually transports Gaussian noise to variational autoencoder latents along linear paths. Both endpoints, however, concentrate in thin spherical shells, and Euclidean chord leaves those shells even when preprocessing aligns their radii. By decomposing each latent token into radial and angular components, we show through component-swap probes that decoded perceptual and semantic content is carried predominantly by direction, with radius contributing much less. We therefore project data latents onto fixed token radius, use the radial projection of Gaussian noise as the spherical prior, finetune the decoder with the encoder frozen, and replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation. The resulting geodesic paths stay on the sphere at every timestep, and their velocity targets are purely angular by construction. Under matched training, the method consistently improves class-conditional ImageNet-256 FID across different image tokenizers, leaves the diffusion architecture unchanged, and requires no auxiliary encoder or representation-alignment objective. Project Website: https://aligning-latent-geometry.github.io Equal Contribution Preprint.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Diffusion [1, 2] and flow matching [35] have driven recent advances in high-fidelity image generation, predominantly through latent diffusion models that train the generator on the encodings of pretrained variational autoencoder (VAE) [6, 7]. These latent image generators do not model pixels directly. They model the coordinate system produced by the VAE [8]; consequently, the shape of this latent distribution defines the space that the generator must learn to traverse [9, 10]. Standard latent flow matching nevertheless treats this space as Euclidean and transports Gaussian noise to encoded data along straight lines [35, 11]. These assumptions are convenient, but they overlook two structural facts about VAE latents: their tokens concentrate in thin spherical shells, and the decoder reacts mainly to tokens direction rather than its length. We show that flow matching pays for both in image quality. In high dimensions, both the Gaussian noise prior and VAE latents concentrate in thin spherical shells [12]. straight line between two such points cuts through the interior, passing through distances from the origin that neither endpoint distribution actually occupies (Sec. 3.2, Fig. 1a). Within these shells, the decoders output depends mainly on tokens direction, not its length: replacing tokens direction with that of same-class neighbor changes the decoded image about as much as replacing the whole token, while replacing only its length barely changes it  (Fig. 3)  . Standard linear flow matching ignores both this shell geometry and the dominance of direction over length. The velocity the model is trained to predict decomposes into radial part that changes tokens distance from the origin and an angular part that changes its direction; near the endpoints, the radial part accounts for roughly half or more of the total  (Fig. 4)  . The cost is measurable: at SiT-B/2 on FLUX.2, vanilla-linear flow matching trains more slowly and attains worse FID [13] after the same training budget than its spherical counterpart (Fig. 6, Tab. 2). Existing geometry-aware alternatives do not supply spherical latent for an existing pretrained VAE. Riemannian flow matching [14] in the feature space of frozen DINOv2 encoder [15] obtains spherical structure but requires an auxiliary encoder at every training and inference step. Hyperspherical methods learn sphere-constrained encoder from scratch [1618] or apply projection only at autoregressive inference [19]. Representation-alignment methods add losses to the VAE [10], to diffusion training [20], or to both [21]; they reshape the latent distribution rather than the geometry of the flow path through it, and require an auxiliary encoder during training. We project each latent token onto sphere at the encoder output of an existing pretrained VAE, finetune only the decoder for few epochs, and replace linear interpolation with spherical linear interpolation, or slerp, between projected endpoints  (Fig. 1)  . Because both endpoints of each token lie on the same fixed-radius sphere, the slerp arc between them stays on the sphere at every timestep, so the training target only changes the tokens direction, never its length. The diffusion architecture is unchanged, no auxiliary encoder is needed at training or inference, and the projection works with both representation-aligned [10, 21] and non-aligned [8] tokenizers. On ImageNet-256, the spherical-slerp method improves FID across FLUX.2, VA-VAE, and REPA-E FLUX.1 tokenizers under matched guidance, and the gain carries from SiT-B to SiT-XL backbones (Tab. 5). decoder-swap control rules out decoder finetuning as the explanation: swapping decoders between the vanilla and spherical pipelines degrades FID in both directions (Tab. 9), showing the learned flow is tied to its latent geometry. Our contributions are: (i) we identify radial-shell mismatch in latent flow matching and quantify how much of its training target is spent on radial motion; (ii) we introduce token-wise spherical projection for pretrained VAE latents with decoder-only finetuning; (iii) we train flow matching along slerp paths, with velocity targets tangent to the sphere and integration that keeps samples on it; and (iv) we show consistent ImageNet-256 gains across three tokenizer families and two model scales under matched protocols.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Hyperspherical latent spaces. L2-normalizing embeddings onto fixed-radius hypersphere is standard structural choice in discriminative representation learning [22, 23]. On the generative side, Davidson et al. [16] introduce sphere-constrained VAE latent by replacing the Gaussian prior and 2 posterior with the von Mises-Fisher distribution, and Xu and Durrett [17] apply the same distribution to mitigate posterior collapse in text VAEs. For continuous-token image generation, Ke and Xue [19] apply fixed-radius projection to the VAE latent to stabilize variance under classifier-free guidance (CFG) in an autoregressive decoder. In concurrent work, Yue et al. [18] train an encoder that maps images uniformly onto sphere and generate by decoding random sphere points, bypassing diffusion entirely. These methods either modify the VAE training objective [16, 17] or skip flow matching as the generator [19, 18]; none studies flow matching on the induced sphere, which is the setting we take up. Riemannian and manifold flow matching. Generative modeling on manifolds was first approached through continuous normalizing flows [24, 25] and score-based diffusion [26, 27], which replace Euclidean drift and Brownian noise with their Riemannian counterparts and integrate along geodesics. Riemannian flow matching [14] extends the simulation-free flow matching framework [35, 28] to this setting, specifying conditional vector fields along geodesic interpolants and projecting velocities onto the tangent space. Davis et al. [29] reparameterize categorical distributions onto the positive orthant of sphere and train with closed-form slerp geodesics, the clearest precedent for slerp as training-time path rather than sampling-time interpolator. Zaghen et al. [30] introduce curvaturedependent Jacobi-field penalty for Riemannian flow matching, and Kumar and Patel [15] apply reweighting on the sphere induced by the final LayerNorm of frozen DINOv2 encoder. Riemannian flow matching has seen limited use on image generation because it requires target latent that already lies on manifold; our spherical projection supplies such latent space from standard pretrained VAE without retraining the encoder. Representation-space diffusion. Another line trains the generator directly in the feature space of frozen representation encoder such as DINOv2 [31]. Kumar and Patel [15] train flow matching on the sphere induced by the LayerNorm in such an encoder, and Zheng et al. [32] pair frozen DINO, SigLIP, or masked autoencoder encoder with trained decoder. Another variant keeps the VAE but adds an alignment loss that pulls the generators intermediate features toward frozen representation encoder, either during VAE training [10], during diffusion training [20], or jointly [21]. All of these options add training-time dependency on an auxiliary encoder, and the frozen-feature-space variants require running that encoder at inference as well. Our spherical projection imposes this sphere structure through geometric constraint on the VAE latent, composes with both representationaligned [10] and non-aligned [6, 8] tokenizers, and adds no auxiliary encoder at inference. Latent-space structure versus reconstruction fidelity. Xu et al. [9] and Yao et al. [10] observe that tokenizer reconstruction quality [33, 6] is weak predictor of downstream diffusion generation quality, and that what governs trainability is the structure of the latent distribution. Xu et al. [9] quantify this decoupling across published autoencoders; Qiu et al. [34, 35] identify sampling-error robustness as the relevant axis; Yao et al. [10] call the phenomenon the reconstruction-generation optimization dilemma. Structural interventions proposed so far include spectral shaping of the latent [36], equivariance regularization [37], end-to-end joint training [21], semantic regularization at scale [38], and non-variational tokenizers with discriminative latents [39, 40]. Our spherical projection is geometric intervention in the same family: it constrains the support of the latent space rather than reshaping its spectrum or aligning it to an external target.

sections.title: 3 Methodology
sections.content: 3.1 Flow Matching in Latent Space We adopt linear-path latent flow matching as our baseline; the rest of this section examines its geometric assumptions. pretrained autoencoder maps an image to latent z1 = E(x) Rdhw with one token in Rd per spatial position, and decoder inverts the mapping. Flow matching [35] learns velocity field vθ(zt, t, y) that transports Gaussian prior z0 (0, I) to data along the linear interpolation zt = (1 t) z0 + z1, [0, 1], (1) with conditional velocity ut = z1 z0 and objective (2) We use the Scalable Interpolant Transformer (SiT) [11] as the backbone. The linear interpolation in Eq. 1 implicitly treats the latent space as Rd with the standard Euclidean structure, an assumption we examine in Sec. 3.2. The two endpoints of the flow-matching path of Eq. 1 are, in these coordinates, the noise sample z0 (0, I) at = 0 and an encoded data latent z1 at = 1. This distinction matters because raw encoder coordinates need not match the coordinates in which the noise prior and flow path are defined. We use this mean-radius expression for the Ez02 = analytical Gaussian rows in Tab. 1; see Sec. A.1 for the derivation. The data endpoint has the same kind of radial concentration. In practice, the Kullback-Leibler (KL) term in the VAE objective [41] does not enforce this. Downstream latent pipelines also apply fixed preprocessing before flow training. We therefore report tokenizer norms both raw and after the preprocessing used by the vanilla baseline (Fig. 2a, Tab. 1). Per-token norms concentrate tightly across all three tokenizers (CV 0.23), and concentration is preserved through preprocessing (raw and processed CV agree within 0.02). After preprocessing, FLUX.2 and VA-VAE sit close to the = 0.35). For z0 = r0 ˆz0 and z1 = r1 ˆz1, (1 t)z0 + tz12 = (1 t)2r2 (4) Independent directions in these token dimensions have small expected cosine, so for r0 r1 = the midpoint norm is close to R/ 2 on average, and empirically the midpoint moves substantially inside the endpoint shell. If r0 and r1 differ, the same path also sweeps between the two shells (Fig. 2a). 1 + 2t(1 t)r0r1ˆz0, ˆz1. 0 + t2r2 Linear paths deviate up to 1.4σ (FLUX.2), 1.8σ (VA-VAE), and 2.5σ (REPA-E FLUX.1) from the nearest endpoint  (Fig. 2)  , placing supervision on latents the training distribution rarely produces. Slerp keeps zt = throughout the flow. To test the decoders sensitivity to direction versus radius, we swap one component between sameclass latents. For an anchor token za = ra ˆza and the same-position token zn = rn ˆzn from same-class neighbor, we form rn ˆza (anchor direction, neighbor radius) and ra ˆzn (anchor radius, neighbor direction), then decode. Both hybrids use real same-class components. Keeping the anchor direction (radius swapped to the neighbor) leaves the decoded image close to the anchor, whereas keeping the anchor radius (direction swapped) moves it almost as far as replacing the whole latent with the neighbor, an asymmetry visible on both LPIPS [42] and DINOv2 distances  (Fig. 3)  . Thus the decoder is much more sensitive to direction than to radius. 4 Linear flow matching allocates substantial supervision to radial motion, component to which the decoder is less sensitive. Decomposing the per-token velocity target ut = zt into radial and tangential components in each tokenizers flow-training coordinates yields an endpoint-dependent radial share. It is about 50% at both endpoints for FLUX.2 and VA-VAE, and reaches about 90% at the noise (Tab. 1, Fig. 4). Slerp endpoint for REPA-E FLUX.1, whose data shell radius falls farthest below on the sphere makes it identically zero by construction. The observed performance gap is consistent with this cost: under the matched protocol, vanilla-linear flow matching trains more slowly than spherical-slerp and attains worse FID after the same training budget (Tab. 2). This mismatch can be addressed either at the path level or the latent-support level. path-level decomposition can avoid the inward chord dip by separating angular and radial motion, but it still keeps radius as supervised prediction target. Since our component-swap probes indicate that decoded content is much more sensitive to direction than to radius, this radial target may require additional normalization or scheduling to avoid competing with angular supervision. We instead remove the radial degree of freedom at its source: project encoder outputs onto fixed-radius sphere so that both endpoints, and the slerp geodesic between them, lie on the same sphere. 3.3 Spherical Latent Spaces We constrain the VAE latent space to fixed-radius hypersphere by inserting token-wise L2 projection between the encoder and decoder. Given pretrained encoder with latent dimension and spatial resolution w, define π : Rd d1( z/z and apply it independently at each spatial position: d) by π(z) = zi,j π(zi,j) = (i, j) {1, . . . , h} {1, . . . , w}. (5) zi,j zi,j , Each token then satisfies zi,j = matches the concentration radius of standard Gaussian in dimensions, aligning the projected latent scale with the noise prior; the full latent tensor lives on product of copies of d1( d). This setting differs from prior hyperspherical VAE work in two respects: we constrain existing pretrained Gaussian VAEs with hard projection rather than training an encoder from scratch, and we keep the downstream flow matching model rather than replacing it with direct decoding from the sphere. d. The radius We freeze the encoder, insert the projection at the encoder output, and finetune only the decoder and discriminator for the tokenizers used in the generation experiments: FLUX.2 [8] (f =8), VA-VAE [10] (f =16, DINOv2-aligned), and REPA-E FLUX.1 [21] (f =8). The reconstruction objective retains the original pixel L1, LPIPS, and patch-level adversarial losses [43, 33]; the KL term is dropped, since the encoder is frozen and the projected latent is deterministic. For VA-VAE the DINOv2 alignment terms have zero gradient once the encoder is frozen, so they are removed; the alignment learned during pretraining persists in the frozen encoder weights. We finetune for five epochs on ImageNet and report the reconstruction tradeoff in Tab. 3. Tokenizer reconstruction quality is weak predictor of downstream diffusion FID across published autoencoders [9, 34, 10]; Skorokhodov et al. [36] trace this to structural properties of the latent (a) Per-token norm along linear, shell, and slerp paths for representative tokenizers. Lines average 2048 pairs; bands show 1σ; the horizontal reference is the fixed spherical radius. (b) Off-shell distance for each path, measured in standard deviations from the nearest endpoint shell. Larger values indicate latent regions rarely occupied by either endpoint; slerp remains at the fixed spherical radius. Figure 2: Linear paths can dip away from the endpoint shells; shell paths interpolate token radii; spherical-slerp stays on the fixed-radius sphere at every timestep. Figure 3: Angular/radial decoder sensitivity. Swapping radius with same-class neighbor preserves the original decode, while swapping direction moves toward the neighbor; markers average 1024 anchors and shape encodes the ablation condition. rather than decoder fidelity. The radial-shell gap is structural property of the same kind: feature of the latent that affects flow matching while leaving reconstruction quality close to the finetuned vanilla control. We therefore report rFID and FID separately and quantify the tradeoff in Sec. 4. The component ablation (Fig. 3, with population-mean substitute and per-sample distributions in Figs. 7 to 9) shows the decoder reads direction far more strongly than radius, so fixing the radius discards the component the decoder is less sensitive to. The projection is applied only inside the VAE: the diffusion model (SiT) sees the spherical latents as ordinary vectors in Rd and requires no architectural changes; no auxiliary encoder is used during diffusion training or inference. This distinguishes the construction from methods that achieve spherical latent geometry by diffusing in the feature space of frozen DINOv2 [15, 31], which run the auxiliary encoder on every generated sample. With both endpoints fixed on d1( d), the remaining design choice is the transport between them. 3.4 Transport on the Sphere With both endpoints on d1( d), slerp gives the shortest geodesic between them and stays on the sphere for all t. We compare it with linear and shell paths, which leave the fixed-radius sphere, to separate the effect of geodesic transport from the projection itself. The standard Gaussian prior d, but its samples do not lie exactly on the sphere. For the spherical path, concentrates near radius we remove only this radial fluctuation: for each token we sample ϵ (0, Id) and set z0 = ϵ ϵ . (6) By rotational invariance of the isotropic Gaussian, ϵ/ϵ2 is uniform on d1, so z0 Uniform(S d1( d)); see Sec. A.2. The spherical noise endpoint is thus the angular component of Figure 4: Radial share of the flow-matching velocity target, computed in each tokenizers flowtraining coordinates. Linear paths spend substantial supervision on radial motion, where the decoder is less sensitive; slerp on the sphere has zero radial velocity by construction. the same Gaussian prior used by the Euclidean baseline. The resulting uniform spherical distribution is also the standard prior used in Riemannian flow matching and directional statistics [14, 44]. Linear path (baseline). The Euclidean interpolation zt = (1 t) z0 + z1 from Sec. 3.1 applies to spherical latents without modification: both endpoints are on the sphere but the path leaves the sphere at intermediate times. This baseline isolates the effect of the spherical constraint from the effect of geometry-aware transport. Shell path. Without requiring the spherical VAE constraint, each endpoint is decomposed into direction and magnitude, = ˆz with ˆz = z/z and = z, and the two are interpolated separately  (Fig. 5)  : ˆzt = slerp(ˆz0, ˆz1; t), rt = (1 t) r0 + r1, zt = rt ˆzt. (7) Because rt moves linearly from r0 to r1, the path avoids the inward chord dip of Euclidean interpolation (Fig. 2a). However, it still preserves radius as supervised component of the flow target. The model is asked to learn both angular motion, which our component-swap probes indicate is more relevant to decoded content  (Fig. 3)  , and radial motion, which is less decoder-sensitive. Without separate normalization or schedule, the joint objective may allocate substantial capacity to the radial component even though it contributes less to the decoded image. Spherical projection takes the complementary approach: rather than balancing angular and radial targets, we remove the radial degree of freedom before flow training, so the resulting slerp target is purely angular. Figure 5: Shell-decomposed path: radius and direction interpolated separately. Slerp path. When both endpoints lie on d1( interpolation geodesic [45]. Writing ˆz = z/z so that = ω = arccosˆz0, ˆz1, the geodesic is d), we interpolate along the spherical linear ˆz, and given angular separation zt = slerp(ˆz0, ˆz1; t) = (cid:18) sin((1 t) ω) sin ω ˆz0 + sin(t ω) sin ω (cid:19) ˆz1 , (8) the unique shortest path between z0 and z1 on the sphere for ω < π [46], staying on the sphere at all times. Following Chen and Lipman [14], the conditional velocity field is the time derivative of the geodesic projected onto the tangent space TztS d1( d): zt, dt ut = ut, zt zt2 zt. In exact arithmetic the slerp time derivative already lies in Tzt d1( d), so the projection on the target acts as numerical safeguard against finite-precision drift. Its more substantive role is on the model output: the network vθ has no architectural tangency constraint, so the same projection is applied before the squared-error loss to enforce that the learned field is tangent and to guarantee sphere-preserving sampling [14, 28]. The resulting target is purely angular  (Fig. 4)  . ut ut (9) For the slerp path, the training loss is the flow-matching objective with both the target and model velocity projected to the tangent space: Lslerp = Et, z0, z1 (cid:104) Πzt vθ(zt, t, y) Πzt ut2(cid:105) , Πzt = v, zt zt2 zt. (10) At inference we apply the same tangent projection to the model velocity and integrate with the exponential map: zt+t = expzt(Πzt vθ(zt, t, y) t) stays on the sphere by construction and has closed-form trigonometric expression, adding negligible cost. This sampler is matched to the slerp training target. Along slerp path with endpoint angle ω, the target velocity is constant-speed ω. single exponential-map step with perfect predictor moves by tangent vector of norm ω and therefore lands exactly on the same slerp curve at time + t; an geodesic arc length Euler step followed by radial projection stays on the same great circle but advances only by arc length (cid:0)t ω arctan(t ω)(cid:1). sections.title: 4 Experiments
sections.content: We evaluate the spherical-slerp method against the vanilla-linear baseline under matched training, asking whether the latent-geometry gain holds across tokenizer families and backbone scales. Experimental Setup. We hold the architecture, data, training budget, and evaluator fixed so that observed differences are attributable to the latent geometry and transport path. We use ImageNet-256 class-conditional generation [47] with the SiT family [11]: B/2 and XL/2 on FLUX.2, B/1 and XL/1 on VA-VAE, and B/2 on REPA-E FLUX.1, adjusting patch size with each tokenizers downsampling factor so that all settings yield 256 tokens per 256 256 image. Models train from scratch for 80 epochs at batch size 256 in bfloat16 with AdamW [48]. Latents are precomputed once per VAE after the tokenizer-specific preprocessing used by the vanilla flow baseline; the spherical variant applies the token-wise projection in those same coordinates before decoder finetuning and flow training. The encoder, and spherical projection are frozen throughout diffusion training. Key ablations are in the supplementary. All reported generation FID numbers are FID-50K, computed on 50K generated samples against the ImageNet-256 training reference. Final generation results use 250 sampling steps; training-curve and latent-path ablations use the same evaluator with 50 sampling steps. Training and sampling protocol Table 2: Latent-support and transport-path ablation on SiT-B/2 with the FLUX.2 VAE. Reconstruction cost of spherical VAEs. Though spherical projection introduces modest reconstruction cost, relative to the matched finetunedvanilla (FT-vanilla) VAE, the downstream generation performance improves noticeably (Tab. 2). Downstream generation always decodes through the corresponding spherical decoder whenever spherical latents are used, so the gain cannot be attributed to using higher-capacity decoder (Tab. 9). Instead, the comparison isolates the effect of changing the latent support and transport geometry. Linear Gaussian Vanilla Shell Vanilla Gaussian Spherical Uniform Linear Spherical Uniform Slerp Euler Euler Euler Exp. map 26.35 27.26 22.85 20.55 off off off on CFG= 1.0 Tan. proj. Sampler Latent FID Prior Path VAE Tokenizer Table 3: ImageNet-256 validation rFID. Spherical uses the fixed-radius projection with decoder/discriminator finetuning; finetuned vanilla (FT-vanilla) is the no-projection control. Transport path comparison. On SiT-B/2 with the FLUX.2 VAE, the spherical construction, comprising the fixed-radius projection, the uniform spherical prior, and the spherical-decoder finetune, explains most of the gain over vanilla-linear, and the slerp path on top gives the best observed result (Tab. 2). The Spherical-Linear row isolates this construction under Euclidean transport; the Spherical-Slerp row adds the geometry-matched path. Shell-decomposed transport on vanilla latents, by contrast, does not improve the baseline. Avoiding the inward chord dip alone is therefore insufficient: with vanilla latents the network is still trained to predict both angular and radial velocity under single objective. Component-swap probes show that decoded content is far more sensitive to angular direction than to radius  (Fig. 3)  , so the radial target competes with the more generation-relevant angular one; coexisting cleanly would require separate normalization, reweighting, or scheduling. Spherical-slerp removes the conflict by training only on tangent, angular velocity, and reaches the target FID in fewer training steps  (Fig. 6)  . FLUX.2 VA-VAE REPA-E FLUX.1 1.072 1.064 0.824 0.486 1.116 0.759 0.186 0.298 0. FT-vanilla Spherical Original 8 16 8 32 32 16 rFID Generation across tokenizers and scales. The spherical-slerp gains hold across tokenizer families, model scales, and guidance settings. It improves matched-CFG FID on FLUX.2 and VA-VAE at both and XL scales, and on REPA-E FLUX.1 at every tested guidance setting (Tab. 5). The tokenizers differ in objective, channel count, and downsampling factor, so the gain is property of the latent geometry rather than of particular VAE. Vanilla-linear Spherical-slerp Method 9.15 8. CFG= 1.0 CFG= 1.5 3.22 2.91 Table 4: epochs on SiT-B/2 with the FLUX.2 VAE. Setting FID IS Tokenizer Scale CFG Linear Slerp Rel. Linear Slerp FLUX.2 VA-VAE B/2 XL/ B/1 XL/1 REPA-E FLUX.1 B/2 1.0 1.5 1.0 1.5 1.0 1.5 2. 1.0 1.5 1.0 1.5 2.0 26.14 9.99 13.00 4.32 26.99 10.86 6.84 12.16 4. 38.00 13.83 7.58 20.21 22.7% 52.5 6.29 37.0% 141.8 10.71 17.6% 3.95 85.4 8.6% 208.1 21.96 18.6% 49.0 7.81 28.1% 120.7 5.46 20.2% 199.6 10.36 14.8% 3. 93.0 8.2% 227.9 26.07 31.4% 42.9 6.88 50.2% 118.3 5.43 28.4% 204.2 66.5 169.7 102.3 238.9 58.2 143.4 231.6 107.3 263. 63.1 172.1 274.0 Scaling. The XL-scale rows in Tab. 5 show that the B-scale recipe transfers to larger backbones. At CFG= 1.0, spherical-slerp improves FLUX.2 XL/2 by 17.6% and VA-VAE XL/1 by 14.8%; at CFG= 1.5, it remains ahead on both tokenizer families. The gains hold without changing the diffusion architecture or adding an auxiliary encoder during diffusion training or inference. The matched-protocol comparison in Sec. 4 fixes the training budget at 80 epochs to control compute across tokenizers and scales. To confirm that the spherical-slerp advantage is not specific to short-budget training, we extend FLUX.2 B/2 to 200 epochs at the same batch size and otherwise identical settings (Tab. 4). Both methods improve substantially over their 80epoch numbers, and spherical-slerp remains ahead at both guidance settings: 8.35 versus 9.15 at CFG= 1.0 and 2.91 versus 3.22 at CFG= 1.5. The lead persists at the longer training budget, consistent with the trainingcurve view in Fig. 6.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: Figure 6: FID-50K at CFG= 1.0 for the latent-support and transport-path ablation on SiT-B/2 with the FLUX.2 VAE. Spherical-slerp reaches FID= 30 in about 2.2 fewer training steps than vanilla-linear and continues to improve. In this paper, we identify geometric mismatch in latent flow matching: VAE latents and Gaussian noise concentrate on thin shells, while linear paths leave those shells and allocate supervision to radial motion to which the decoder is weakly sensitive. We address this with spherical latent flow matching: project each VAE token to fixed-radius sphere, use projected Gaussian directions as uniform spherical prior, and train along slerp geodesics with tangent velocities, without changing the diffusion architecture or adding an auxiliary encoder. On ImageNet-256, this geometry improves matched-CFG FID across FLUX.2, VA-VAE, and REPA-E FLUX.1, transfers from SiT-B to SiT-XL, and reaches the vanilla-linear FID floor in fewer training steps. These results establish latent support geometry as useful design axis; future work should test spherical constraints under stronger compression and design sphere-constrained tokenizers that match the reconstruction quality of unconstrained tokenizers.

With perfect velocity predictor, the exponential-map sampler is therefore exact along the slerp curve. Euler step followed by radial projection. One-step deficit. Both samplers stay on the great circle through zt and zt+h. The exponential-map step advances by arc length hθ0, while the projected Euler step advances by arctan(hθ0). sections.title: B Implementation Details
sections.content: B.1 Slerp Numerical Handling Per-token slerp treats each spatial positions d-dimensional vector as point on d1( d). The implementation handles three regimes by the angle ω between the unit-normalized endpoints. For ω [104, π 0.1] the standard slerp formula applies. For ω < 104 the path falls back to linear interpolation followed by renormalization to avoid 0/0 in sin ω. For ω > π 0.1 the path traces cos(πt) ˆx0 +sin(πt) ˆn, an arbitrary great circle from ˆx0 through an orthogonal unit ˆn to ˆx0. Cosines are clamped to [1 + 106, 1 106] before arccos and norms are floored at 108. For random unit vectors with {16, 32}, the per-token cosine concentrates near zero with scale 1/ d, so the small-angle and antipodal branches are correctness insurance rather than hot paths during training. B.2 Training Hyperparameters Hyperparameters are grid-searched at SiT-B with the FLUX.2 tokenizer and applied unchanged at SiT-XL and to the VA-VAE and REPA-E FLUX.1 tokenizers, with only the timestep shift adapted per tokenizer. The linear and spherical recipes share these hyperparameters and differ only in the path (Euclidean linear vs. slerp), the latent (raw VAE vs. projected to d1( d)), and the slerpspecific design choices already documented in Sec. 3.4 (tangent projection on the model output, exponential-map sampler). Table 6 lists the resulting configuration. Table 6: Training hyperparameters used for all linear-baseline and spherical-slerp runs in the main paper. Only the timestep shift varies across tokenizers; all other settings are shared. Setting Value Optimizer Weight decay EMA decay Mixed precision Global batch size Training duration Learning rate LR schedule Gradient clip Time sampling Timestep shift Training-curve FID-50K 50K samples, 50 sampling steps, CFG= 1.0, every 50K steps Final FID-50K AdamW [48], β1=0.9, β2=0.999 0 0.9999, averaged from step 0, no warmup bf16 (parameters and EMA in fp32; forward/backward autocast) 256 80 epochs 4104 constant, no warmup 1.0 logit-normal 4.63 (FLUX.2, REPA-E FLUX.1), 1.0 (VA-VAE) 50K samples, 250 sampling steps, CFG values per Tab. 5 B.3 Compute All experiments run on NVIDIA H200 GPUs. SiT-B runs use 2 GPUs each; SiT-XL runs use 4 GPUs each. Token counts are equalized across tokenizers by pairing the patch size with the VAE downsample factor: SiT-B/1 and XL/1 for VA-VAE (downsample factor 16), and SiT-B/2 and XL/2 for FLUX.2 and REPA-E FLUX.1 (downsample factor 8). All settings yield 256 tokens per 256256 image. B-scale run completes 80 epochs in approximately 6.5 wall-clock hours on 2H200 ( 13 H200-hours); an XL-scale run completes in 16.8 hours on 4H200 ( 67 H200-hours). B.4 Code and Checkpoint Release We will release training and evaluation code, finetuning configurations for the FLUX.2, VA-VAE, and REPA-E FLUX.1 spherical decoders, and the trained SiT-B and SiT-XL flow-matching checkpoints reported in Tab. 5.

sections.title: C Additional Experiments and Ablations
sections.content: C.1 Core Recipe Checks Recipe ablations are run on FLUX.2 as the canonical tokenizer; cross-tokenizer transfer of the resulting recipe at B-scale across FLUX.2, VA-VAE, and REPA-E FLUX.1 is reported in the main paper (Tab. 5). The matched-recipe decoder finetune does not by itself explain the gain in Tab. 2: applying it to the vanilla decoder under vanilla-linear flow moves FID only 26.35 26.95 (Tab. 9), so the remaining improvement comes from the latent geometry rather than from the finetune. Table 7: Selected recipe checks on SiT-B/2 with the FLUX.2 spherical VAE and slerp path (80 epochs, FID-50K (50 steps) at CFG= 1.0). Each row changes one method-relevant factor from the default.

sections.title: Configuration
sections.content: Spherical-slerp default Uniform time sampling Unit-radius sphere No tangent projection FID 20.55 23.86 22.08 25.79 C.2 Recipe Parity Matching the training recipe improves the vanilla baseline, but the spherical-slerp geometry remains better (Tab. 8). Table 8: Recipe parity on SiT-B/2 with the FLUX.2 VAE (80 epochs, FID-50K (50 steps) at CFG= 1.0). Configuration Training recipe FID Baseline Vanilla-linear Vanilla-linear Matched Spherical-slerp Matched 34.30 26.35 20.55 C.3 Decoder-Flow Coupling The gain comes from the spherical projection, not the finetune compute. On FLUX.2 B/2, matchedrecipe decoder finetune without the projection remains tied with the vanilla baseline, while swapping decoders between vanilla and spherical flows degrades both pairings (Tab. 9). The flow learns velocity field calibrated to specific latent geometry and the decoder learns to invert that geometry; the two co-adapt and cannot be substituted independently. Table 9: Decoder-flow coupling on FLUX.2 SiT-B/2 (CFG=1.0). Latent Path Decoder Finetune Prior Vanilla Vanilla Spherical Vanilla Spherical Linear Vanilla Linear Vanilla Slerp No Gaussian Matched Gaussian Spherical Matched Sph. Uniform Linear Slerp Spherical Matched Gaussian Vanilla Sph. Uniform No FID 26.35 26.95 20.55 112.17 43.17 C.4 Scaling with Number of Function Evaluations (NFE) Table 10 sweeps the number of function evaluations (NFE) on VA-VAE B/1 at CFG = 1.0. Sphericalslerp wins at every tested NFE, with the gap largest at NFE = 20 (19.84 FID) and narrowing to 5.30 at NFE = 500; the advantage holds across sampling budgets, not just at low step counts. C.5 Direction-vs-Radius Component Ablation Figure 3 substitutes same-class partners direction or radius for the anchors. As stronger baseline, Fig. 7 substitutes the population-mean direction and radius across the whole ImageNet validation 17 Table 10: Number of function evaluations (NFE) scaling on ImageNet-256 with VA-VAE B/1 at CFG= 1.0. NFE Vanilla-linear Spherical-slerp

sections.title: Gap
sections.content: 20 50 100 500 70.44 31.99 28.25 27.26 50.60 23.88 22.04 21.96 19.84 8.11 6.21 5.30 split, isolating whether radius information is carried by single dataset-wide constant. Each tokenizer reports LPIPS and DINOv2 cosine similarity to the original decode for four conditions: original, keep direction (own direction with substitute radius), keep radius (substitute direction with own radius), and full substitute. For FLUX.2 and REPA-E FLUX.1 with the population-mean substitute, keep-direction reaches LPIPS 0.040.08 and DINOv2 cosine 0.940.97: replacing the radius with single global constant leaves the decode visually almost unchanged. VA-VAE is somewhat further (LPIPS 0.18, DINOv2 0.86) but still recognizable. Keep-radius lies at LPIPS 0.880.93 and DINOv2 0.010.02 across all three tokenizers, indistinguishable from the full mean substitute: once the direction is replaced, additionally replacing the radius changes nothing further. The same-class substitute  (Fig. 3)  yields the same pattern: keep-direction stays near the original, while keep-radius nearly matches the full-neighbor swap. Figures 8 and 9 plot the per-sample distributions underlying these averages, one point per image, x-axis is LPIPS for keep direction, y-axis is LPIPS for keep radius. Every point lies above y=x for all three tokenizers in both substitute regimes; the same-class substitute spreads the radius axis slightly further than the population-mean substitute, most visibly for VA-VAE. Decoded content is carried almost entirely by the angular direction; the decoder appears largely insensitive to radius. Linear flow paths spend substantial velocity on this radial motion  (Fig. 4)  ; slerp on the sphere has no radial component by construction. Figure 7: Angular/radial decoder sensitivity, population-mean substitute. For each tokenizer, markers report mean LPIPS (left) and DINOv2 cosine similarity (right) to the original decode under four conditions: original, keep direction, keep radius, full mean substitute; 1024 images per condition. C.6 Scope of Held-Out Comparisons REPA-E [21] jointly trains the VAE and the diffusion model under representation-alignment objective; matching its compute requires retraining both stacks under that objective, which is out of scope for study that holds the tokenizer fixed and modifies only the latent geometry. Kumar and Patel [15] train flow matching directly in the frozen DINOv2 feature space, different tokenizer regime that requires running the encoder at inference. The setting studied here (a geometric constraint on the latent of standard pretrained VAE, with no auxiliary encoder at training or inference time) uses less compute than these two methods at comparable diffusion-model scale. Figure 8: Per-sample direction vs. radius sensitivity, population-mean substitute. Each point is one image; x-axis: LPIPS after replacing radius with the population mean (direction kept); y-axis: LPIPS after replacing direction with the population mean (radius kept). Panels: FLUX.2, VA-VAE, REPA-E FLUX.1. Figure 9: Per-sample direction vs. radius sensitivity, same-class partner substitute. Axes as in Fig. 8; the substitute is same-class partners radius (x-axis) or direction (y-axis).

sections.title: D Qualitative Results
sections.content: D.1 Class-Conditional Samples Figure 10 shows class-conditional samples from the spherical-slerp SiT-XL/2 model with the FLUX.2 tokenizer. Each panel is 44 grid of 16 samples for single ImageNet-1k class. D.2 Same-Class Component Swaps Figure 3 averages LPIPS and DINOv2 distance over 1024 anchor-neighbor pairs. Figures 11 to 13 show representative pairs for each tokenizer. Columns are Original 1 (anchor), Angular 1 + Radius 2 (anchor direction with neighbor radius, the keep-direction hybrid), Angular 2 + Radius 1 (anchor radius with neighbor direction, the keep-radius hybrid), and Original 2 (neighbor). Across all three tokenizers, the keep-direction column is visually nearly indistinguishable from the anchor, while the keep-radius column resembles the neighbor. D.3 Reconstructions The rFID gaps in Tab. 3 are not visible to inspection. Figures 14 to 16 compare reconstructions from the original tokenizer (Vanilla), the matched-compute vanilla decoder finetune (Vanilla FT), and the 19 (a) Chickadee (19) (b) Box turtle (37) (c) Alligator lizard (44) (d) Toy terrier (158) (e) Chesapeake Bay retriever (209) (f) Newfoundland (256) (g) Cardigan (264) (h) Cliff dwelling (500) (i) Fountain (562) (j) Moving van (675) (k) Piggy bank (719) (l) Rain barrel (756) (m) Stone wall (825) (n) Ice cream (928) (o) Mushroom (947) Figure 10: Class-conditional samples from spherical-slerp SiT-XL/2 with the FLUX.2 tokenizer. Each panel: 16 samples (44) for one ImageNet-1k class. Figure 11: FLUX.2 same-class component swaps. Columns: anchor (Original 1), keep-direction hybrid (Angular 1 + Radius 2), keep-radius hybrid (Angular 2 + Radius 1), neighbor (Original 2). Each row is one (anchor, neighbor) pair. spherical decoder finetune (Spherical FT) on eight ImageNet classes. At 256256 per tile, the three reconstructions are visually near-identical, as expected for high-fidelity tokenizers.

sections.title: E Scope and Future Directions
sections.content: This work focuses on isolating the effect of latent geometry in controlled class-conditional ImageNet256 setting. We therefore keep the diffusion backbone, training budget, evaluator, and tokenizerspecific preprocessing fixed, and study three pretrained VAE/tokenizer families with native token dimensions = 16 and = 32. This controlled setting lets us attribute the observed gains to the spherical latent support and geodesic transport rather than to architectural or data changes. An interesting direction is to study how the same geometric constraint behaves for more compressed tokenizers, where the radial degree of freedom may play larger role in reconstruction. Our experiments use matched sampling budgets and guidance settings to compare latent geometries directly. broader solver-efficiency study, including adaptive ODE solvers, lower-NFE samplers, and distillation-based acceleration, is complementary to our contribution. Since spherical-slerp keeps the latent trajectory on the fixed-radius manifold throughout sampling, future work could explore whether this structure can be exploited by specialized integrators or training-time acceleration methods. Finally, we evaluate on ImageNet-256 to provide clean and widely used benchmark for classconditional generation. Extending spherical latent flow matching to text-conditioned generation, 21 Figure 12: VA-VAE same-class component swaps; columns as in Fig. 11. higher resolutions, non-square aspect ratios, and jointly trained encoder-decoder tokenizers would further test the generality of the approach. These directions do not require changing the central formulation: with an encoder whose outputs are projected token-wise onto fixed-radius sphere, the flow model is trained along the corresponding spherical geodesics. 22 Figure 13: REPA-E FLUX.1 same-class component swaps; columns as in Fig. 11. 23 Figure 14: FLUX.2 reconstructions. Columns: original image, original (Vanilla) decoder, matchedcompute vanilla decoder finetune (Vanilla FT), and spherical decoder finetune (Spherical FT). Rows: sheepdog, tabby, airliner, beach wagon, crate, matchstick, seat belt, orange. 24 Figure 15: VA-VAE reconstructions; columns and rows as in Fig. 14. 25 Figure 16: REPA-E FLUX.1 reconstructions; columns and rows as in Fig. 14.
